title: Object types — shape your data
instruction:
In TypeScript, every object has a type that describes its shape. The most direct way is an inline type annotation — but for objects you'll use more than once, a `type` or `interface` is cleaner.

Create typed objects for a product catalog.
successMessage: Both objects satisfy the `Product` type. TypeScript checks that every required field is present with the correct type. Missing a field or getting a type wrong is immediately an error.

title: Structural typing — shape matters, name doesn't
instruction:
TypeScript uses **structural typing** — a value satisfies a type if it has the right shape, regardless of how it was created. This is different from Java/C# where you must explicitly declare that you implement an interface.

See structural typing in action.
successMessage: Neither `circle` nor `rectangle` explicitly implements anything — they just happen to have the right methods. TypeScript checks the shape at compile time. This is called 'duck typing': if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck.

title: Index signatures — dynamic key-value maps
instruction:
When you need an object with dynamic keys (you don't know the keys in advance), use an index signature `[key: string]: ValueType`.

Build a cache and a settings store using index signatures.
successMessage: Index signatures allow any string key. The value type is enforced — `pageMetrics["/home"] = "many"` would be a type error. `Object.entries()` returns `[string, number][]` because TypeScript knows the value type.

title: Object spread — merge and override
instruction:
The spread operator `...` creates new objects by merging existing ones. Later properties override earlier ones. TypeScript infers the merged type.

Build a configuration merge system.
successMessage: Spread merges objects — later properties win. `devConfig` keeps the default `apiUrl` because `development` doesn't override it. This pattern is everywhere in configuration management.

title: Nested objects — complex data shapes
instruction:
Objects can be nested. TypeScript tracks types at every level — you get autocomplete and type checking even deep inside nested structures.

Model an e-commerce order with nested data.
successMessage: TypeScript tracks types through every level of nesting. `order.customer.email` is `string`, `order.items[0].unitPrice` is `number` — no manual type checks needed at any depth.

title: Object.keys, entries, values — iterate safely
instruction:
`Object.keys()`, `Object.values()`, and `Object.entries()` let you iterate over an object's properties. TypeScript types these returns carefully.

Build a settings inspector using Object methods.
successMessage: `Object.entries()` returns `[string, string][]` here — TypeScript infers the value type from the object. The key type is always `string` (TypeScript's limitation — keys are always widened to `string`).

title: Computed property names — dynamic keys
instruction:
Square bracket notation lets you create objects with dynamic key names. Combined with TypeScript, you can create type-safe lookup maps.

Build a dynamic response code registry.
successMessage: `Record<HttpMethod, string>` ensures every method in the union has an entry. Add `"OPTIONS"` to the `HttpMethod` union and TypeScript immediately tells you to add it to both records.

title: Object.assign — merge with type safety
instruction:
`Object.assign()` merges objects in place. TypeScript infers the result type. However, spread syntax is generally preferred for its clarity and immutability.

Compare Object.assign with spread syntax.
successMessage: Both `Object.assign({}, ...)` and spread `{ ...base, ... }` produce a new object. Prefer spread — it's more readable and the intent is clearer. Note: `base` was not mutated because we passed `{}` as the target.

title: Destructuring objects from function returns
instruction:
Functions that return objects can be destructured at the call site. This is the standard pattern for returning multiple values from a function.

Build a validator that returns both a result and an error.
expectedOutput: Invalid: false, errors: Missing @ symbol, Missing domain extension
successMessage: Destructuring with renaming (`isValid: v1`) lets you avoid name conflicts when calling the validator multiple times. TypeScript knows each destructured variable's exact type.

title: Freezing objects — true runtime immutability
instruction:
`Object.freeze()` prevents runtime mutation. When combined with `as const`, TypeScript also makes the type fully immutable (literal types, not just `readonly`).

Create truly immutable configuration objects.
successMessage: `as const` narrows all values to their literal types — `OK` is `200` not `number`. `StatusCode` becomes the union `200 | 201 | 204 | 400 | 401 | 403 | 404 | 500`. The `Record<StatusCode, string>` ensures all codes are handled.

title: Pick specific properties with object spread
instruction:
You can extract specific properties from an object without destructuring the rest. This is useful when preparing data for APIs that only need certain fields.

Extract public fields from a user object.
successMessage: Destructuring with `...rest` splits the sensitive fields from the safe ones. `toPublicProfile` cannot accidentally include `passwordHash` because it's destructured out — TypeScript's type system mirrors this safety.

title: Object type guards — narrow at runtime
instruction:
A type guard function checks an object's shape at runtime. TypeScript narrows the type after the check, giving you safe access to specific properties.

Write type guards for different API response shapes.
successMessage: Inside `if (isSuccess(response))`, TypeScript narrows `response` to `SuccessResponse` — `.count` is accessible. In the `else` branch, it's `ErrorResponse` — `.error` and `.code` are accessible. Perfect type safety.

title: Satisfies operator — validate without widening
instruction:
The `satisfies` operator (TypeScript 4.9+) validates that a value matches a type without changing the inferred type. This preserves literal types while still type-checking the structure.

Use satisfies for a theme configuration.
successMessage: `satisfies` validates the structure (all values must be `Color`) while keeping the inferred type narrow (each property is its literal type). This is useful for configs where you need both type safety and exact value types.
